speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape number six, and we are interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon. Now, we finished the last tape, when you spoke about the fact that the people from the first transport were taken to extermination.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how-- how did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a-- a little before, when I was in this children block, and we looked-- we saw the crematorium the whole time. And we even make jokes, like children always make jokes. Now there's white smoke. It means they're the fat people. Or now it's dark smoke. Now these are burning something. So we knew what is going on. We didn't know exactly how it's going on. Well, children are very curious.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew exactly how it's going on-- about the gas?

speaker: interviewee
text: No a little later. At that time, we just knew that they're going to the gas. And we knew also that older people say, no. It's not true. At night, they take them out from the camps. But we knew that they are gassed, that a crematorium was there. We made even jokes about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the smoke?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the whole time the smoke and flames. In-- now, the first transport of Hungarian Jews arrived in April. It was shortly after the annihilation of the Czech transport. How we-- we know that they were, they went to the gas, the night before-- before we separated, we were on this children block, where we slept. That means there was a block for day, and we slept in another block. And we were making jokes like all children. We knew that this one, my friend, is from the old block. So we said, oh tomorrow you go to heaven. And please keep me a job, a light job, and you will go to Saint Peter. And we will-- you-- I-- you will-- will be banging on the gong. The gong was a sign in Auschwitz every morning. There was a gong and the bell for--

speaker: interviewer
text: For the appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- yeah. And-- and we said, you will be also a heater-- that was our job-- in the heaven. So we knew exactly what will happen the next day. But as children, even a day before their death, we made these kind of jokes. And next day, of course, they took the children, and the whole transport away, the one who came before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also the  children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we visited-- yeah. We visited Fredy Hirsch. Because we had a special ceremony. On the 7th of March was [INAUDIBLE] of our children's room in Theresienstadt. And for this date, we called it a big day. Like scouts, it's a big day. And we wanted to invite Fredy Hirsch to celebrate with us. And I went to him. And he was very sad and upset-minded. And he excused himself. He can't come with us to celebrate it. And there was also his secretary, Hanka. Tante Hanka, we called her. And both of them were very sad. Later on, we heard that he took some tablets and killed himself in spite that he had the chance from the SS to be saved. Much later on, I spoke this through-- through [INAUDIBLE], Zuckerman. They were sonderkommandos. And we were later neighbors of theirs. And he told me exactly how Fredy was brought-- brought into the crematorium. And he himself--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was dead already?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was dead. They couldn't-- tried to save him, but they couldn't. And he himself put him into the oven. And he remembered it because it was a single--

speaker: interviewer
text: Person.

speaker: interviewee
text: --person. Usually they were working very hard in tens and hundreds and thousands. But he was single. And he remembered. They think it was through Zuckerman. And we also heard later on some kapos who could move back and forwards like kapos. They were [INAUDIBLE], a special compound where they've done all kinds of work for the Germans. They went from one camp to another. And that was a kind of possibility of information. So they told us exactly what happened, even some letters people wrote in the crematorium, in the place where they had to take off the clothes. And they were smuggled out. And this letter came to the camp. And of course, much later, I knew exactly what was going on. Because I visited several times the gas chambers. And the sonderkommandos were very near to us. And they told us how they behaved. They sang before they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Czechs, you were speaking about the Czechs?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who went to the gas chambers, our transport, our friends. They-- three songs which, the Hatikvah, the Czech hymn, and the international. So they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were socialists.

speaker: interviewee
text: From all kinds of-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sang in the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they knew exactly-- before they went into the gas chamber, of course, in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I mean, I can later describe you all about it. So that was-- that we knew what is happening. We knew that we have so-in-so many more months, our time, more or less finished on the 20 of June. Because we arrived, according to the 20 of June. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Half a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just counting the days, and we knew exactly that will happen. Because the scribe or the man who was working with the registration, his name was Kapo Franc, a French little Jew. He had all the [INAUDIBLE] tags. And he was the kapo of this [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: He wrote down--

speaker: interviewee
text: He wrote down all-- and we knew we have these six months. Now what happened in April, two interesting things happened was special. I was still the heater of the chimneys. There were not so many people. Because many of the men, of course, with the gas, the atmosphere was different. The use lead, after Fredy Hirsch, was also somebody still from Thereseinstadt separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it change your attitude-- were you sad? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we were very sad. And Fredy Hirsch was a demigod for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and he disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had saved us. Why? Because his way of education, to keep us trim, to make exercises, is-- and if we were a bit dirty, he looked in our nails. And he forced us to wash ourselves, even with snow. And I remember we had not a towel, a group of 20 children had a handkerchief. There was hardly any water running. But his drilling, in a-- in a way, oppression drilling, saved our lives. Because in Auschwitz, of course, the people who somehow survived had a better chance to survive if they looked more or less still human, or according to the German or Nazi conception of cleanliness, human, and tidiness. And if you looked somehow starting to be what we called a muselmann, or you had a little rash, that would mean you go to the gas chambers in the next-- or also you would be hit, or you got much worse treatment than somebody who still looked half human.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's, thanks to Fredy, one of the-- You learned--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the many reasons why we survived is because he drilled us to keep fit, even in these circumstances. Because who wanted in Auschwitz to wash himself with snow, or look [NON-ENGLISH]. And he very much stressed this point. Now afterwards was another [INAUDIBLE] that was the head of the block, [? Sepel. ?] And [NON-ENGLISH]. But we somehow continued our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were not like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't like before?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very sad. Miriam Edelstein, it was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wife of Jacob Edelstein, was also the mother of the block after Hanka who went to gas. And her husband, of course, was in Birkenau-- sorry, in Auschwitz 1, in the bunker number 11, which was one of the worst bunkers, where they kept people, prisoners from Gestapo in bunker 11. 50 years later I visited it. And he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was coming and going--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. He was there. But once was a commission, and people think it was Eichmann who came there. And he promised Miriam, because he knew her well from Theresienstadt. She asked when I will see my husband. And he said, yes. You will see him. You will meet him. And he kept his word. But only they met on the 20 of-- after the 20 of June, in the crematorium they met. There were some executed, some about 12 or so people. And again, I knew exactly from the man who was there, it's an execution, single execution, done by a decree from Berlin. Apropos, from Berlin always came also all these preparations, let's say. The sonderkommando told me, don't worry. We will know exactly when, and so on. Because we are getting a telephone [? that ?] Berlin-- prepare the ovens and transport of so-in-so, many people, is on the way, or this-and-this will happen. So they knew exactly when to start the ovens for new transports. But when there were single people, like partisans, or in my time, a very famous scientist, they kept even this [? alien ?] or a special way in Germany, and let them work as long as they needed them. But then they came singly to Auschwitz, straight to the crematorium. And there they were shot. And the German doctor had to sign that he was shot, and this kind of document has to be sent to Himmler or whoever it was in this-- so it was this single treatment. Because most people didn't get this single treatment by a-- a letter or telephone call from Berlin. And this single treatment had to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Signed.

speaker: interviewee
text: --signed by a German doctor in the crematorium. And my friend, he was in most of these executions, and he told me the details. But maybe I will tell it to you later on. Now what happened in April was special. And Miriam Edelstein gave us something like a quart or a half kilo of [NON-ENGLISH], flour. And we had to start again the fire in the oven. And that was a whole business. Because you had to prepare it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it was already warm. You didn't need it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was very difficult, and we had to ask for the match, only much later, let's say, at 10 o'clock from this SS man. I don't know what it was. But now we came very early. And we wanted to do a fire, to prepare the fire. And no-- no matches. But there was tiny little reddish ashes from yesterday. And as if by a miracle, we went whoo, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you succeeded in making fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: We succeeded to making fire. And also it was dangerous that SS men shouldn't see us. And we made fire, and we baked maybe one or two matzahs. So this was in Pesach. I think it was April in '44. It was really like a miracle. Apropos, there were many people who prayed in-- in-- even in Birkenau. I remember one friend had only [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH]. They didn't have both. But it was very dangerous. And they handled-- gave it from one to another, the older people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people were still religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some. The religious people were still, some of them were still religious. And Yom Kippur and so on, they made-- that was later on, and-- in about August. I remember they prayed in main lager. But now coming back, that was the situation in April '44. I remember seeing the first transport from Hungarian Jew. Altogether when the transport arrived, it was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was dangerous to go out of the block. You could be shot. You should-- you could be killed. But we children didn't care so much. And as our block was very near to the railways, so we looked. And we saw the very first time these strange people. They looked-- they were-- had long, long journeys. And we couldn't understand them. They spoke a strange language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they spoke Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They spoke Hungarian. And then we saw-- we knew exactly from the way where they went, if they go to the chimneys or to the sauna. It depends when. But sometimes there was a little road between the day lager, the main lager, and the C lager. There was a little path where we marched, and so on. And you could go around via crematorium number 3 and 4. These were the old-fashioned crematorium which had one-- two long chimneys. Today they are called different numbers. And the gas chambers were not underneath, in a cellar, but the same floor. And there was another crematorium, 1 and 2, which was the more modern one, where the crematoriums were underneath and also the room where they had to undress. Anyhow, we could know where they-- where they are going. So that was the beginning of the Hungarian transport. And then they came in a kind of covered--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Influx. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I've never seen such a thing, one after another, day and night, they were rolling transports. Sometimes in between there was a transport from Paris. And that we know that people, let's say, later on even from Lodz, the people who came from the east, they had nothing anymore. And the people who came from Paris, or even from Hungaria, they had a little food. And when the kapo from the sonderkommando, when I was sitting in the block of the sonderkommando, and the kapo came in, and said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], there is-- is coming a transport from [INAUDIBLE], it's Paris. He wanted to encourage them to go to-- to the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: To work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we told them, he said, Jews from Paris. Because if he wouldn't say, people would try to get out of-- of the job. I mean, as much as they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? If they come from Paris, they are-- maybe they have jewels?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have-- they have better things. They had better food. They have better clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they can take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sonderkommando--

speaker: interviewee
text: The sonderkommando could take-- I don't know to tell you know or later. They had much better condition, and we children somehow profited from it. But maybe I will go back now to our family camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we were only these people who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in May--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were actually something very, very special.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, came another, a huge transport, two or three from Theresienstadt. [? It ?] brought 7,500 Jews. They wold come in different number. In Auschwitz, have a different number for men and women. And the men after 200,000, the series was over. So they gave number A and so on. So now they started with A, which meant actually 200,000 and.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I understand. I've seen people with A--

speaker: interviewee
text: A and B.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some have triangles. But A means after May, 200,000 for men. Women, let's say my mother got, when I got 106-- 168, a woman had 73,000. It was a different kind of numbering. Anyhow, then people came. And they were so-- the crematorium and sauna were so overbooked that these people was in-- were in our camp, in this familienlager, maybe a-- for a week or even longer, until they could go to the sauna and get tattooed. Because it was a tremendous pressure in the crematoriums with-- with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarian Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: --thousands of Hungarian Jews which arrived in May, June, and July, I think. So they came also, knew stories from Theresienstad. We heard new things about Theresienstad. And something now about the illogical way of life. In Theresienstad, everyone tried to get out of a transport to the east. Because it was a bet. Nobody knew what will happen. But the illogical thing was that when we were-- when we arrived in Auschwitz or in Birkenau, the kapo said, we are the strong one. You will perish. I have a better chance. I have food. You are nobody, nothing. You will go tomorrow in the crematorium. And these people went before us. The same story happened with gypsies later on. They were making fun of us. Gypsy children to gypsy-- to Jewish children. Ah, you see, that it's fabric, they make of you bread or marmalade. And they went to the chimneys before us. So there was something completely illogical. All the people who arrived--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no logic in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: --much later went to the chimneys. And we who have been there over a year somehow survived. So you could never use logic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the system didn't work later on, like half a year, and after half a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It worked. Now, when it came to be a half a year, and we were afraid what will happen now.

speaker: interviewer
text: June, it was June '44 for you, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: June '44. We were very afraid. Something happened. They came-- Dr. Mengele with-- anyhow, a car came and took Miriam Edelstein, and Aryeh who was with us children, and Mrs. Oliner, her mother, and some other prominent Jews, about 10, 11, I think, and took them away. And we didn't hear from them anymore. But later on, of course, I heard from my sonderkommando, they took them. And Jacob Edelstein met them in the crematorium. And they were shot first them, and-- and Aryeh and Jacob Edelstein. They were executed in-- in the crematorium. That be-- that we know. Afterwards, we were waiting what will happen, what will happen. Nothing happened. But in the beginning--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the others were taken to the-- to crematorium? The others who came later?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. They were, this other 7,500, they were tattooed, came with us. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: And lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, filled up.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very crowded, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crowded. And there were so many Hungarian Jews going to the crematorium. Very little of these women and we saw all of them went to this Zigeunerlager-- C camp next to us, the C compound, it was next to the B compound. And they went also to a new compound they started to build. It was called Mexico. It's nothing to do with Mexico. But they called it Mexico. They built huge barracks. But there were not many people in this Mexico. But they only built-- it was really for all these woman-- a huge barrack. And some went to the FKL Frauen, near. It was an only a women compound. Where most of the barracks were even from stones. We were in some wooden barracks, this tiny little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: You are speaking about the Hungarian Jews, yes? But they were put-- some of them were put in barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this C, main-- the ones who survived, of course. Most of them went straight to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. They were chosen to work? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were chosen to work. And they suffered terribly. Because, you see, we got everything slowly, slowly, the dehumanization. It took slow the path, Theresienstadt, and even there we had the children. And at Auschwitz we had also better conditions. So-- and he had some, as-if training, Fredy Hirsch and so on, better food, better clothes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a warmer camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You developed a resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. To the children camp, everyone wanted to go in and warm himself. The other barracks were not so warm, and had better condition. I remember every morning we gave coffee in the children block-- in the children block to the people. There was a special wagon for the corpse. So call it normal, dying people. It was full of dead corpse. And the boy who had this job, [INAUDIBLE] is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he came to our block in the morning, and we gave him hot coffee. What does it mean? You had coffee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, not real coffee it's cikoria coffee. But it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but it was night.

speaker: interviewee
text: --something hot to drink.

speaker: interviewer
text: With sugar?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I don't remember, maybe saccharine. But anyhow, hot drink, it was saving somebody's life. And we could provide it for him. I mean this children block, to him this is terrible job. Now, I speak again about '44. After they took Aryeh and Miriam and some other people, Mrs. Oliner, her mother. And we were left. And we knew something is happening. And really then they came big selection. The German changed their politics. Usually everyone goes, or had to go like before. But now we know there was a tremendous demand from the Wehrmacht who were working. The people--

speaker: interviewer
text: To work?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work, to-- to-- in the factories, in Buna, in the railways to repair after the bombing. So they make selection of men and-- and women. And there was a selection of men who were between, let's say, 17 and 40. And women without children between more or less the same age. Now, I remember there were cases that-- because in our camp were babies that some women wanted to smuggle out their tiny little babies. And Mengele came there. I was in this-- near to this group and could hear and said, what will happen to us? It was women with children. And Mengele said, don't worry. We'll come to a place, and he described exactly, because I saw it later on, exactly the gas chambers and the room where you take off your clothes. It's white-washed, and clean, and quiet. You will come there. And it will be fine for your children. In another group of people who went out of the camp, after the selection, a mother with-- tried to smuggle out a little baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of Auschwitz. But the baby started to cry. Mengele heard it, and that was her end, of course. She was left. Now what happened? The people who went through the selection, let's say my mother couldn't pass it. She was only 40. But was so weak. And in Auschwitz, a woman of 40 looked like 65. She couldn't pass it. My sister passed. She was 20. But I somehow-- I was a very clever and very like an animal in danger. I was very alert. I pushed her through another-- through another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I told her, look. You have a sister. She didn't want to live anymore. I said, no. You have to live. You have a daughter in Israel. You have to live. So I-- she went through the selection. My father was at that time 41 or so. So he didn't pass it. And there was no chance. He developed a heart--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in a bad condition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was also looking-- it wouldn't be-- later on when the-- when we went on the two death march, I said something, which is unbelievable. When I saw-- when the people were killed, and I saw all shot. And I saw all the brains, the gray brain on the street. I said, thank god that my father went to the gas chamber. He surely wouldn't be able to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to survive this terrible thing which I had. And he would be shot. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your mother passed.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother passed the selection. And I know that she came later on to Stutthof, where she--

speaker: interviewer
text: With your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sister, both got [NON-ENGLISH], typhoid. And two or three weeks, they came in the block from typhoid, they survived the typhoid. But they didn't have anymore food. It was [INAUDIBLE]. So they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: They starved.

speaker: interviewee
text: They starved to death just these two weeks before the liberation. Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: We should-- we have. I'm sorry. We shall stop now. And we shall soon go on.
